freshness.reason: Dual-source opportunity-roi-v3 run 2026-06-12. Xiaohongshu corpus collected 2026-06-12 (56 posts/50 authors); re-check market+community Aug-Oct 2026.

assumptions: Single focused project: a from-scratch LLM inference engine, taken deep on the scheduling stack and then benchmarked, across devices, against the real production systems (vLLM, SGLang, llama.cpp).
Inference systems only. No training, distillation, or interview-gate track — depth on one engine.

Outperformance thesis: do not try to beat vLLM/SGLang on datacenter GPUs at scale. Beat them in a specific, defended regime — Apple Silicon, memory-constrained (16GB), and low-to-moderate concurrency — where general-purpose datacenter engines are weak or absent.
Multi-device benchmark: Apple Silicon M4 16GB (MPS/Metal) and a short-lived rented CUDA GPU; the same engine, the same seeded traces, the same metrics on both.
Execution window Jun 22 - Sep 13, 2026, 20 h/week, ~17 committed + ~3 reserve.
Depth over breadth: implement named papers (Orca, Sarathi-Serve) plus one outperformance optimization from a paper, all measured end to end.

candidates.label: LLM inference engine & serving-systems engineer
candidates.thesis: Build a from-scratch LLM inference engine — paged KV cache, Orca continuous batching, Sarathi-Serve chunked prefill, SLO-aware admission — then benchmark it across devices (cloud CUDA and Apple Silicon) against vLLM, SGLang, and llama.cpp. Finally, implement one optimization from a paper and outperform the production baseline in a defended regime: Apple Silicon, memory-constrained, and low-to-moderate concurrency. One focused project, named papers implemented, measured against the real thing, with a concrete win where it counts.
skill_axes: LLM inference internals: paged KV cache, continuous batching, chunked prefill
Cross-device benchmarking vs vLLM / SGLang / llama.cpp (CUDA + Apple Silicon)
Targeted optimization (KV quantization/offload, speculative decoding) for outperformance in a regime
same_person_problem_set: Implement PagedAttention, Orca batching, and Sarathi-Serve chunked prefill and prove parity with a reference
Benchmark the engine against vLLM, SGLang, and llama.cpp on the same traces across CUDA and Apple Silicon
Implement a paper-backed optimization and beat the production baseline in a specific, defended regime
candidates.proof_artifact: A from-scratch inference engine with paged KV, continuous batching, and chunked prefill; a reproducible cross-device benchmark vs vLLM, SGLang, and llama.cpp; and one optimization that outperforms the production baseline in a defended regime.
falsification_conditions: Inference-systems roles stop rewarding from-scratch engine work when re-checked Aug-Oct 2026
The engine cannot reach a defensible fraction of vLLM/SGLang throughput on CUDA, or beat llama.cpp on Apple Silicon
No paper-backed optimization yields a reproducible win in any defended regime

candidates.label: On-device / Apple-Silicon inference engineer
candidates.thesis: Same engine, but center the whole story on Apple Silicon: exploit unified memory and Metal to beat llama.cpp on concurrency and long-context in 16GB. Narrower, but a very clean hardware-advantage story.
same_person_problem_set: Beat llama.cpp on the M4 with continuous batching + quantized/offloaded KV
candidates.proof_artifact: An Apple-Silicon-native engine that beats llama.cpp on concurrency and long-context in 16GB.
falsification_conditions: On-device-only reads as too narrow for the broader systems roles

roles.name: ML Systems Engineer
roles.summary: Makes inference/training fast and correct: serving stacks, quantization, performance. The niche home base.

roles.name: AI Infrastructure Engineer
roles.summary: Platforms for the ML lifecycle: serving infra, data/training platforms, reliability.

roles.name: AI Software Engineer
roles.summary: Builds AI-powered products: evals, LLM integration, backend glue.

roles.name: Software Engineer
roles.summary: Generalist builder: services, tooling, reliability. The widest entry door.

roles.name: AI Research Engineer
roles.summary: Turns research into systems. Practitioner evidence: PhD-gated, crowded for new grads.

workflow_stages.name: Serving
workflow_stages.description: Inference runtime: batching, KV cache, latency.

phases.title: From-scratch inference engine · 12 weeks
phases.outcome: A from-scratch LLM inference engine — paged KV cache, Orca continuous batching, Sarathi-Serve chunked prefill, SLO-aware admission — benchmarked across devices against vLLM, SGLang, and llama.cpp, with one optimization that outperforms the production baseline in a defended regime.
phases.description: Minimal engine + cross-device harness -> paged KV cache -> continuous batching (Orca) + admission -> chunked prefill (Sarathi-Serve) + Pareto -> cross-device benchmark vs vLLM/SGLang/llama.cpp -> outperformance optimization + release.
phases.milestone: Named papers implemented, a reproducible multi-engine multi-device benchmark, and one paper-backed optimization that beats the production baseline in a defended regime.

sections.title: Weeks 1-2 · Minimal engine + multi-device benchmark harness
sections.integration_checkpoint: The streaming API serves the model on both MPS and CUDA; the oracle is frozen; the harness reports baseline metrics reproducibly on both devices.
sections.name: Weeks 1-2 · Minimal engine + multi-device benchmark harness

sections.title: Weeks 3-4 · Paged KV cache (PagedAttention)
sections.integration_checkpoint: Paged decode matches the oracle; the memory study shows reduced fragmentation and higher max concurrency at fixed memory on both devices.
sections.name: Weeks 3-4 · Paged KV cache (PagedAttention)

sections.title: Weeks 5-6 · Continuous batching (Orca) + admission control
sections.integration_checkpoint: Continuous batching matches the oracle per sequence, shows a throughput gain over static batching on both devices, and under overload returns 429 with bounded p99 and no leaked KV.
sections.name: Weeks 5-6 · Continuous batching (Orca) + admission control

sections.title: Weeks 7-8 · Chunked prefill (Sarathi-Serve) + latency-throughput Pareto
sections.integration_checkpoint: Chunked prefill matches the oracle; the Pareto frontier is plotted per device with an identified knee and a stated best-served regime.
sections.name: Weeks 7-8 · Chunked prefill (Sarathi-Serve) + latency-throughput Pareto

sections.title: Weeks 9-10 · Cross-device benchmark vs vLLM, SGLang, and llama.cpp
sections.integration_checkpoint: A reproducible comparison matrix reports your engine vs vLLM, SGLang, and llama.cpp across CUDA and Apple Silicon, from checked-in configs, with a defended target regime for the optimization.
sections.name: Weeks 9-10 · Cross-device benchmark vs vLLM, SGLang, and llama.cpp

sections.title: Weeks 11-12 · Outperformance optimization + portfolio release
sections.integration_checkpoint: The optimization preserves quality and produces a reproducible win over the production baseline in the defended regime; the report and README tell the end-to-end story from checked-in configs.
sections.name: Weeks 11-12 · Outperformance optimization + portfolio release

lessons.title: Weeks 1-2 · Minimal engine + multi-device benchmark harness
lessons.problem: Before any optimization you need a correct, instrumented baseline that runs on every target device: a single-request decode loop, a deterministic oracle to check optimizations against, and one benchmark harness that measures the same metrics on Apple Silicon and on a cloud CUDA GPU.
lessons.why_it_matters: Every later module — and the whole outperformance claim — is judged against this harness. A cross-device, reproducible measurement rig is the part that separates a real systems project from a demo.
lessons.build_it: Implement a single-request decode loop and an OpenAI-compatible streaming endpoint with sampling, stop conditions, and cancellation.
lessons.use_it: Drive it with the seeded traces and the cross-device harness; check parity against the oracle.
lessons.ship_it: Publish the module with tests, the measurement, and a short design note; update the engine README.

exercises.prompt: Implement a single-request decode loop and an OpenAI-compatible streaming endpoint with sampling, stop conditions, and cancellation.
exercises.completion_gate: The streaming API serves the model on both MPS and CUDA; the oracle is frozen; the harness reports baseline metrics reproducibly on both devices.

exercises.prompt: Author seeded traces (short, mixed, bursty); record single-request baselines on Apple Silicon and on a rented CUDA GPU.

tests_and_measurements: A minimal engine with a streaming API, a frozen oracle, and a cross-device (Apple Silicon + CUDA) benchmark harness.
lessons.proof_artifact: A minimal engine with a streaming API, a frozen oracle, and a cross-device (Apple Silicon + CUDA) benchmark harness.
lessons.objective: Build a single-request decode loop over a small model with an OpenAI-compatible streaming API; a deterministic token oracle; and one device-agnostic benchmark harness (throughput, TTFT, TBT, p99, peak memory) that runs on Apple Silicon (MPS) and cloud CUDA from the same seeded traces.
lessons.exact_inputs: The previous module's artifact, a small model (Qwen2.5-0.5B / Llama-3.2-1B), the frozen oracle, and the seeded traces; the production baselines for benchmark modules.
lessons.architecture_config:
engine: Python over PyTorch; device backends: MPS (Apple Silicon) and CUDA (cloud); model: Qwen2.5-0.5B / Llama-3.2-1B
API: OpenAI-compatible /v1/completions streaming; sampling, stop conditions, cancellation
oracle: deterministic seeded path; harness: throughput, TTFT, TBT, p99, peak memory; seeded traces (short, mixed, bursty)
lineage: nano-vllm and tiny-llm studied for a dependency-light, inspectable loop
lessons.abstraction_level: Own the decode loop, streaming API, oracle, and the device-agnostic harness. Delegate matmul/attention to PyTorch (MPS/CUDA). Keep everything single-request here so every later optimization can be proven against this baseline on both devices.
implementation_steps: Implement a single-request decode loop and an OpenAI-compatible streaming endpoint with sampling, stop conditions, and cancellation.
Freeze a deterministic oracle fixture (prompt -> token IDs) used by every later module for parity.
Build a device-agnostic benchmark harness (throughput, TTFT, TBT, p99, peak memory) selectable between MPS and CUDA.
Author seeded traces (short, mixed, bursty); record single-request baselines on Apple Silicon and on a rented CUDA GPU.
lessons.exit_gate: The streaming API serves the model on both MPS and CUDA; the oracle is frozen; the harness reports baseline metrics reproducibly on both devices.

lessons.title: Weeks 3-4 · Paged KV cache (PagedAttention)
lessons.problem: Contiguous KV allocation wastes memory through fragmentation and caps concurrency. PagedAttention fixes this with fixed-size KV blocks and per-request block tables — the biggest lever on how many requests fit in memory, and the foundation of the memory-constrained outperformance story.
lessons.why_it_matters: On a 16GB device KV is the real ceiling, not weights. A paged allocator is what later lets the engine fit more concurrency / longer context than baselines — the core of beating production systems in the memory-constrained regime.
lessons.build_it: Implement a fixed-size KV block allocator with a free list and per-request block tables.
lessons.use_it: Drive it with the seeded traces and the cross-device harness; check parity against the oracle.
lessons.ship_it: Publish the module with tests, the measurement, and a short design note; update the engine README.

exercises.prompt: Implement a fixed-size KV block allocator with a free list and per-request block tables.
exercises.completion_gate: Paged decode matches the oracle; the memory study shows reduced fragmentation and higher max concurrency at fixed memory on both devices.

exercises.prompt: Measure utilization and max concurrency at fixed memory (contiguous vs paged) on Apple Silicon and CUDA.

tests_and_measurements: A PagedAttention-style KV cache with a contiguous-vs-paged memory study on both devices and oracle parity.
lessons.proof_artifact: A PagedAttention-style KV cache with a contiguous-vs-paged memory study on both devices and oracle parity.
lessons.objective: Implement a PagedAttention-style KV cache: a fixed-size block allocator, per-request block tables, allocation/free, reference-counted shared blocks, and attention over paged blocks; quantify the memory win vs a contiguous baseline on both devices.
lessons.exact_inputs: The previous module's artifact, a small model (Qwen2.5-0.5B / Llama-3.2-1B), the frozen oracle, and the seeded traces; the production baselines for benchmark modules.
lessons.architecture_config:
kv: fixed-size blocks (e.g., 16 tokens/block); per-request block table; free list; reference-counted blocks
attention: gather KV from blocks (paged kernel via FlashInfer/torch, or a correctness gather)
study: contiguous vs paged utilization and max concurrent requests at fixed memory, on MPS and CUDA
paper: vLLM / PagedAttention (SOSP'23)
lessons.abstraction_level: Own the block allocator, block tables, free list, and the paged gather. Delegate matmul/softmax to PyTorch. Prove paged decode matches the oracle token-for-token before measuring memory.
implementation_steps: Implement a fixed-size KV block allocator with a free list and per-request block tables.
Implement attention that gathers KV from non-contiguous blocks; verify parity with the W1-2 oracle.
Add on-demand allocation, free-on-completion, and reference counting for shared blocks.
Measure utilization and max concurrency at fixed memory (contiguous vs paged) on Apple Silicon and CUDA.
starter_interfaces: engine/kv_cache.py     # block allocator + free list + block tables
bench/memory.py        # contiguous vs paged, per device
lessons.exit_gate: Paged decode matches the oracle; the memory study shows reduced fragmentation and higher max concurrency at fixed memory on both devices.

lessons.title: Weeks 5-6 · Continuous batching (Orca) + admission control
lessons.problem: Static batching wastes the device and collapses under load. Orca schedules at the granularity of a single decode iteration, admitting and retiring requests every step; admission control keeps the system within SLO and sheds excess safely.
lessons.why_it_matters: Continuous batching is the single biggest throughput lever (10-20x) and the default in vLLM/SGLang. It is also what lets the engine beat llama.cpp on concurrent throughput on Apple Silicon, where llama.cpp's server batches far less aggressively.
lessons.build_it: Implement an iteration-level scheduler: each step select runnable requests, run one decode iteration, retire finished ones.
lessons.use_it: Drive it with the seeded traces and the cross-device harness; check parity against the oracle.
lessons.ship_it: Publish the module with tests, the measurement, and a short design note; update the engine README.

exercises.prompt: Implement an iteration-level scheduler: each step select runnable requests, run one decode iteration, retire finished ones.
exercises.completion_gate: Continuous batching matches the oracle per sequence, shows a throughput gain over static batching on both devices, and under overload returns 429 with bounded p99 and no leaked KV.

exercises.prompt: Verify per-sequence parity with the oracle; measure throughput/goodput vs static batching on both devices.

tests_and_measurements: An Orca-style continuous-batching scheduler with admission control, oracle parity, and a throughput/goodput study on both devices.
lessons.proof_artifact: An Orca-style continuous-batching scheduler with admission control, oracle parity, and a throughput/goodput study on both devices.
lessons.objective: Implement Orca-style iteration-level scheduling on the paged KV cache — a ragged, padding-free batch whose membership changes every step — plus a bounded queue with token/KV-aware admission, overload 429s, and cancellation cleanup.
lessons.exact_inputs: The previous module's artifact, a small model (Qwen2.5-0.5B / Llama-3.2-1B), the frozen oracle, and the seeded traces; the production baselines for benchmark modules.
lessons.architecture_config:
scheduler: per-iteration admit/step/evict; ragged batch; no right-padding; per-sequence position ids + masks
admission: bounded queue; token-budget + paged-block-availability admission; overload -> 429; per-request timeout + cancellation
study: throughput and goodput vs static batching across seeded traces, on MPS and CUDA
paper: Orca (OSDI'22)
lessons.abstraction_level: Own the scheduler loop, batch membership, padding-free masking, admission arithmetic, and overload behavior, on the W3-4 paged cache. Require parity with the single-request oracle for every sequence before optimizing.
implementation_steps: Implement an iteration-level scheduler: each step select runnable requests, run one decode iteration, retire finished ones.
Build ragged, padding-free batches with per-sequence position ids and masks; free KV on completion.
Add a bounded queue with token/KV-aware admission, overload 429s, timeouts, and cancellation cleanup.
Verify per-sequence parity with the oracle; measure throughput/goodput vs static batching on both devices.
starter_interfaces: bench/throughput.py    # continuous vs static, per device
lessons.exit_gate: Continuous batching matches the oracle per sequence, shows a throughput gain over static batching on both devices, and under overload returns 429 with bounded p99 and no leaked KV.

primary_sources.title: Orca: A Distributed Serving System for Transformer-Based Generative Models

primary_resource.name: OSTEP
primary_resource.section: OSTEP scheduling + concurrency (queues, condition variables) for an iteration-level inference scheduler

lessons.title: Weeks 7-8 · Chunked prefill (Sarathi-Serve) + latency-throughput Pareto
lessons.problem: A long prefill monopolizes a decode step and stalls every other request's tokens, spiking tail latency. Sarathi-Serve splits prefills into token-budgeted chunks and piggybacks decode tokens; sweeping the knobs reveals the latency-throughput Pareto frontier.
lessons.why_it_matters: Chunked prefill is the default in vLLM V1 and the cleanest 'read a paper, extend the scheduler' step. The Pareto study is what lets you say precisely which regime you win in — the backbone of the outperformance claim.
lessons.build_it: Add a per-iteration token budget and split long prefills into chunks; piggyback decodes (stall-free batching).
lessons.use_it: Drive it with the seeded traces and the cross-device harness; check parity against the oracle.
lessons.ship_it: Publish the module with tests, the measurement, and a short design note; update the engine README.

exercises.prompt: Add a per-iteration token budget and split long prefills into chunks; piggyback decodes (stall-free batching).
exercises.completion_gate: Chunked prefill matches the oracle; the Pareto frontier is plotted per device with an identified knee and a stated best-served regime.

exercises.prompt: Plot the latency-throughput Pareto frontier per device and identify the knee and the regime you serve best.

tests_and_measurements: A Sarathi-Serve chunked-prefill scheduler and a per-device latency-throughput Pareto characterization with a stated best regime.
lessons.proof_artifact: A Sarathi-Serve chunked-prefill scheduler and a per-device latency-throughput Pareto characterization with a stated best regime.
lessons.objective: Implement Sarathi-Serve chunked prefill + stall-free batching, then sweep the token-budget/batch/chunk knobs to map the latency-throughput Pareto frontier on Apple Silicon and CUDA.
lessons.exact_inputs: The previous module's artifact, a small model (Qwen2.5-0.5B / Llama-3.2-1B), the frozen oracle, and the seeded traces; the production baselines for benchmark modules.
lessons.architecture_config:
prefill: split into token chunks under a per-iteration token budget; mix chunked prefill with ongoing decodes (stall-free)
study: sweep token budget / max batch / chunk size; plot throughput vs p99 latency (the Pareto frontier) per device
paper: Sarathi-Serve (OSDI'24), extending Orca
lessons.abstraction_level: Own the chunking policy, the per-iteration token budget, the stall-free mixing of prefill chunks with decodes, and the knob sweep/analysis, on the Orca scheduler. Prove parity with the oracle, then characterize the frontier.
implementation_steps: Add a per-iteration token budget and split long prefills into chunks; piggyback decodes (stall-free batching).
Verify chunked prefill produces identical tokens to the oracle.
Sweep token budget / max batch / chunk size; record throughput and p99 at each point on both devices.
Plot the latency-throughput Pareto frontier per device and identify the knee and the regime you serve best.
starter_interfaces: bench/pareto.py        # knob sweep + frontier, per device
lessons.exit_gate: Chunked prefill matches the oracle; the Pareto frontier is plotted per device with an identified knee and a stated best-served regime.

lessons.title: Weeks 9-10 · Cross-device benchmark vs vLLM, SGLang, and llama.cpp
lessons.problem: An engine is only credible against the real thing. This module runs the same model and the same seeded traces through vLLM, SGLang, and llama.cpp across devices, and places your engine on the same axes — honestly establishing where you stand and where you can win.
lessons.why_it_matters: The apples-to-apples, multi-engine, multi-device comparison is the single most convincing artifact for an inference role. It also pins down the exact regime to target in Week 11-12.
lessons.build_it: Stand up vLLM and SGLang on a rented CUDA GPU and llama.cpp on the M4 (and CUDA) with the same model and quantization.
lessons.use_it: Drive it with the seeded traces and the cross-device harness; check parity against the oracle.
lessons.ship_it: Publish the module with tests, the measurement, and a short design note; update the engine README.

exercises.prompt: Stand up vLLM and SGLang on a rented CUDA GPU and llama.cpp on the M4 (and CUDA) with the same model and quantization.
exercises.completion_gate: A reproducible comparison matrix reports your engine vs vLLM, SGLang, and llama.cpp across CUDA and Apple Silicon, from checked-in configs, with a defended target regime for the optimization.

exercises.prompt: Identify the target regime: where you match/beat (Apple Silicon, memory-constrained, low concurrency) and where you don't (CUDA at scale).

tests_and_measurements: A reproducible cross-device benchmark matrix vs vLLM, SGLang, and llama.cpp, with a defended target regime.
lessons.proof_artifact: A reproducible cross-device benchmark matrix vs vLLM, SGLang, and llama.cpp, with a defended target regime.
lessons.objective: Stand up vLLM, SGLang, and llama.cpp as baselines; run the shared seeded traces through all of them and your engine across a rented CUDA GPU and the Apple Silicon M4; report throughput, p99 TTFT/TBT, goodput, and memory; identify the regime where your engine is competitive or ahead.
lessons.exact_inputs: The previous module's artifact, a small model (Qwen2.5-0.5B / Llama-3.2-1B), the frozen oracle, and the seeded traces; the production baselines for benchmark modules.
lessons.architecture_config:
baselines: vLLM (CUDA), SGLang (CUDA), llama.cpp (Apple Silicon + CUDA); your engine on both devices
traces: shared seeded (short, mixed, bursty, long-context, low-concurrency)
metrics: throughput, p99 TTFT/TBT, goodput, peak memory; configs + run cards checked in
output: a comparison matrix and a stated target regime for the optimization
lessons.abstraction_level: Own the harness, the comparison methodology, and the analysis. Use the production engines as external baselines — same model, same traces, same metrics. Be rigorous and honest about where they win and where they don't.
implementation_steps: Stand up vLLM and SGLang on a rented CUDA GPU and llama.cpp on the M4 (and CUDA) with the same model and quantization.
Run the shared seeded traces through every engine on every device; capture throughput, p99 TTFT/TBT, goodput, memory.
Build the comparison matrix (engine x device x trace x metric) from checked-in configs and run cards.
Identify the target regime: where you match/beat (Apple Silicon, memory-constrained, low concurrency) and where you don't (CUDA at scale).
starter_interfaces: bench/baselines/         # vLLM, SGLang, llama.cpp launch + config
bench/compare.py        # engine x device x trace matrix
lessons.exit_gate: A reproducible comparison matrix reports your engine vs vLLM, SGLang, and llama.cpp across CUDA and Apple Silicon, from checked-in configs, with a defended target regime for the optimization.

lessons.title: Weeks 11-12 · Outperformance optimization + portfolio release
lessons.problem: With an honest baseline in hand, implement one paper-backed optimization aimed at the target regime and demonstrate a reproducible win over the production baseline there — then package the whole thing as a portfolio artifact.
lessons.why_it_matters: A concrete 'beat the production system in this regime, here is the number' result is the sentence that gets the interview. It turns a faithful reimplementation into a project with a point of view.
lessons.build_it: Choose the optimization from the menu based on the W9-10 target regime and your hardware.
lessons.use_it: Drive it with the seeded traces and the cross-device harness; check parity against the oracle.
lessons.ship_it: Publish the module with tests, the measurement, and a short design note; update the engine README.

exercises.prompt: Choose the optimization from the menu based on the W9-10 target regime and your hardware.
exercises.completion_gate: The optimization preserves quality and produces a reproducible win over the production baseline in the defended regime; the report and README tell the end-to-end story from checked-in configs.

exercises.prompt: Write the benchmark report and portfolio README; (optional stretch) hand-write one CUDA/Metal kernel on a hot path.

tests_and_measurements: One paper-backed optimization that reproducibly outperforms the production baseline in the defended regime, plus the benchmark report and portfolio README.
lessons.proof_artifact: One paper-backed optimization that reproducibly outperforms the production baseline in the defended regime, plus the benchmark report and portfolio README.
lessons.objective: Pick one optimization from the menu, implement it, and show a reproducible win over the production baseline in the defended regime; ship a benchmark report and portfolio README. Menu (choose one): KV quantization (KIVI 2-bit) for more concurrency/longer context in fixed memory; KV eviction / attention sinks (H2O, StreamingLLM) for bounded long-context KV; unified-memory KV offload (FlexGen-style, Apple-Silicon-native, no PCIe penalty); or speculative decoding (EAGLE / self-speculative) for single-stream latency.
lessons.exact_inputs: The previous module's artifact, a small model (Qwen2.5-0.5B / Llama-3.2-1B), the frozen oracle, and the seeded traces; the production baselines for benchmark modules.
lessons.architecture_config:
target regime (from W9-10): Apple Silicon, memory-constrained (16GB), low-to-moderate concurrency
optimization (pick one):
  - KV quantization (KIVI 2-bit): ~4x KV in fixed memory -> more concurrency / longer context than baselines
  - KV eviction / attention sinks (H2O / StreamingLLM): bounded KV for long/streaming contexts
  - unified-memory KV offload (FlexGen-style on Apple unified memory, no PCIe penalty): larger effective KV than VRAM-bound engines
  - speculative decoding (EAGLE / self-speculative): single-stream latency win where vLLM spec-decode is unavailable (Apple Silicon)
win condition: a reproducible metric where the engine beats the production baseline in the target regime, with parity/quality preserved
lessons.abstraction_level: Own the optimization implementation and the head-to-head measurement against the production baseline in the target regime. Delegate kernels to PyTorch/FlashInfer; an optional hand-written CUDA/Metal kernel is a stretch. Quality must be preserved (parity or a bounded quality delta).
implementation_steps: Choose the optimization from the menu based on the W9-10 target regime and your hardware.
Implement it on the engine; verify output quality is preserved (parity or a measured, bounded delta).
Re-run the benchmark in the target regime; show a reproducible win over the production baseline.
Write the benchmark report and portfolio README; (optional stretch) hand-write one CUDA/Metal kernel on a hot path.
starter_interfaces: engine/opt/             # the chosen optimization (kv_quant / evict / offload / spec)
bench/win.py            # head-to-head vs baseline in the target regime
lessons.exit_gate: The optimization preserves quality and produces a reproducible win over the production baseline in the defended regime; the report and README tell the end-to-end story from checked-in configs.

projects.title: A from-scratch LLM inference engine, benchmarked against production and optimized to win in a regime
projects.positioning_statement: Not a model and not a vLLM wrapper: a from-scratch inference engine, benchmarked head-to-head against vLLM, SGLang, and llama.cpp across cloud CUDA and Apple Silicon, then optimized with a paper-backed technique to outperform the production baseline in a defended regime (Apple Silicon, memory-constrained, low-to-moderate concurrency).
user_advantage: Reuses Jetson/TensorRT/CUDA experience: constrained memory and latency profiles, paged KV, and measured throughput tradeoffs.
Reuses AWS reliability experience: admission control, backpressure, overload behavior, timeouts, and graceful shutdown.
Apple Silicon advantage: an M4 16GB is the exact regime where vLLM/SGLang are weak or absent, so the outperformance story is credible.
projects.research_question: Across vLLM, SGLang, and llama.cpp on cloud CUDA and Apple Silicon, in which regime can a from-scratch engine — with paged KV, continuous batching, chunked prefill, and one paper-backed optimization — match or beat the production baseline, and by how much?
what_you_will_implement: Weeks 1-2: Minimal engine + multi-device benchmark harness — A minimal engine with a streaming API, a frozen oracle, and a cross-device (Apple Silicon + CUDA) benchmark harness.
Weeks 3-4: Paged KV cache (PagedAttention) — A PagedAttention-style KV cache with a contiguous-vs-paged memory study on both devices and oracle parity.
Weeks 5-6: Continuous batching (Orca) + admission control — An Orca-style continuous-batching scheduler with admission control, oracle parity, and a throughput/goodput study on both devices.

Weeks 7-8: Chunked prefill (Sarathi-Serve) + latency-throughput Pareto — A Sarathi-Serve chunked-prefill scheduler and a per-device latency-throughput Pareto characterization with a stated best regime.
Weeks 9-10: Cross-device benchmark vs vLLM, SGLang, and llama.cpp — A reproducible cross-device benchmark matrix vs vLLM, SGLang, and llama.cpp, with a defended target regime.

Weeks 11-12: Outperformance optimization + portfolio release — One paper-backed optimization that reproducibly outperforms the production baseline in the defended regime, plus the benchmark report and portfolio README.
problem_index: Weeks 1-2 Minimal engine + multi-device benchmark harness
Weeks 9-10 Cross-device benchmark vs vLLM, SGLang, and llama.cpp

implementation_references.title: tiny-llm (skyzh)
implementation_references.use: from-scratch serving course in MLX on Apple Silicon (Qwen3)

implementation_references.title: Outcome School — AI & Machine Learning
implementation_references.use: curriculum reference: LLM internals, vLLM serving, inference-platform system design

owned: the inference engine: iteration-level scheduler, paged KV cache, continuous batching, chunked prefill, admission control
the cross-device benchmark harness and methodology
the outperformance optimization (KV quantization/offload or speculative decoding)
delegated: production baselines to vLLM, SGLang, and llama.cpp
low-level Metal/CUDA kernels (one optional hand-written kernel as a stretch)

proof_gaps: No public from-scratch inference-engine artifact yet.
No measured head-to-head benchmark vs production systems.
No demonstrated optimization that beats a production baseline in any regime.
acceptance_criteria: Every batched / paged / optimized path matches the single-request oracle (parity or a measured, bounded quality delta).
A reproducible cross-device benchmark reports the engine vs vLLM, SGLang, and llama.cpp from checked-in configs and run cards.
The latency-throughput Pareto frontier is characterized per device with a stated best-served regime.
The chosen optimization yields a reproducible win over the production baseline in the defended regime.
An architecture report and portfolio README separate owned engine code from delegated kernels and baselines.

resource_decisions.reason: Primary serving-internals reference: vLLM internals, continuous batching, SLOs, and a speculative-decoding server.
Serving-systems vocabulary and benchmarking methodology for the whole engine.

assigned_sections: Ch. 26-32 Concurrency, threads, locks, condition variables — https://pages.cs.wisc.edu/~remzi/OSTEP/
resource_decisions.reason: Concurrency and scheduling primitives for the iteration-level scheduler and admission control.
Block allocation and page-table design, the analog for the paged KV cache.

assigned_sections: Serving systems and the throughput-latency tradeoff — https://hao-ai-lab.github.io/cse234-w25/
resource_decisions.reason: Modern serving systems and the throughput-latency tradeoff.
resource_decisions.resource_name: CSE 234: Data Systems for Machine Learning

assigned_sections: Load, latency percentiles, backpressure, graceful degradation — https://dataintensive.net/
resource_decisions.reason: Reliability under load: percentiles, backpressure, and graceful degradation for admission control.

resource_decisions.trigger: Optional Week 11-12 hand-written CUDA/Metal kernel on a hot path.
resource_decisions.reason: Kernel-level optimization for the optional outperformance-stretch kernel.
Dependency-light, inspectable inference loop as the from-scratch reference.
resource_decisions.resource_name: Karpathy from-scratch LLM repos (nanoGPT, llm.c, llama2.c, nanochat)

cuts.reason: This is an inference-systems project; training a model would displace the engine depth. Separate project.
cuts.revisit_when: A post-training-focused role or a follow-on project.

cuts.item: Interview-gate track (DSA, system design as project units)
cuts.reason: Deleted from the project scope to keep the site focused on one deep engine; interview prep is maintained separately, not as project modules.
cuts.revisit_when: Closer to interview season, outside this project.

cuts.reason: Needs multi-GPU and KV transfer over the network — out of reach for a solo 16GB-Mac + single-GPU-benchmark budget.
cuts.revisit_when: Multi-GPU access and a role that requires disaggregation.

cuts.item: Beating vLLM/SGLang on datacenter GPUs at scale
cuts.reason: Naive and falsifiable; production engines win on their home turf. The defended claim is outperformance in a specific regime (Apple Silicon, memory-constrained, low-to-moderate concurrency).
cuts.revisit_when: Never as framed; the regime-specific claim is the honest one.

cuts.reason: Own the scheduler/KV/admission algorithms; delegate kernels to PyTorch/FlashInfer. One hand-written kernel is an optional Week 11-12 stretch.
cuts.revisit_when: The optimization ships early and reserve hours remain.

compute_policy.canonical_environment: MacBook Pro M4 16GB is the primary development and Apple-Silicon benchmark device (MPS/Metal, small model). A short-lived rented CUDA GPU (e.g., a 24GB Runpod Pod) runs the CUDA leg: your engine plus the vLLM and SGLang baselines. llama.cpp is benchmarked on both the M4 and CUDA.
compute_policy.expected_total_spend: Development is Mac-local and free. Paid compute is the benchmark legs on a rented 24GB CUDA GPU (~USD0.27-0.69/hour); a handful of benchmark hours imply roughly USD15-40. Plan USD20-50 expected, USD150 hard ceiling.
compute_policy.per_run_gate: Cloud runs only after the Mac-local module passes its parity gate; each benchmark run is scripted from checked-in configs and run cards so it is reproducible and short.

execution_timeline.title: 12-week execution timeline: June 22 to September 13, 2026
execution_timeline.capacity_rule: About 17 committed hours/week plus ~3 reserve; ~174h committed with slack for debugging, reruns, and applications.
parallelization_rules: Mac-first: engine and scheduler logic is developed and tested on the M4 (MPS) with a small model; the same code runs on CUDA.
Cloud is benchmark-only: a short-lived rented CUDA GPU runs vLLM/SGLang and the CUDA leg of the comparison, never routine dev.
Each module gates the next: paged KV before batching; batching matches the oracle before chunked prefill; an honest baseline before the optimization.
